title: Top K Frequent (bucket sort)
code: public int[] TopKFrequent(int[] nums, int k)
for (int f = buckets.Length - 1; f >= 0 && result.Count < k; f--)

frames.caption: Goal: the k most frequent values. Build a frequency map, bucket each value by its count, then sweep buckets high → low until we have k.
frames.note: Sorting the frequencies would be O(n log n); a size-k heap is O(n log k). Bucket sort beats both at O(n) by exploiting one fact specific to this problem: a frequency is a small integer bounded by n, so it can be used directly as an array index instead of being compared. That is the whole trick.

frames.caption: Count nums[0] = 1: freq[1] = 1.
frames.note: Step 1 of every top-K solution is the frequency map: one O(n) pass tallying value → count. A hash map answers "how many times have I seen x?" in O(1) average, so the whole tally is O(n). Everything after this works off the d distinct (value, count) pairs, not the original n elements.

frames.caption: Count nums[1] = 1: freq[1] = 2.
frames.note: Step 1 of every top-K solution is the frequency map: one O(n) pass tallying value → count. A hash map answers "how many times have I seen x?" in O(1) average, so the whole tally is O(n). Everything after this works off the d distinct (value, count) pairs, not the original n elements.

frames.caption: Count nums[2] = 1: freq[1] = 3.
frames.note: Step 1 of every top-K solution is the frequency map: one O(n) pass tallying value → count. A hash map answers "how many times have I seen x?" in O(1) average, so the whole tally is O(n). Everything after this works off the d distinct (value, count) pairs, not the original n elements.

frames.caption: Count nums[3] = 2: freq[2] = 1.
frames.note: Step 1 of every top-K solution is the frequency map: one O(n) pass tallying value → count. A hash map answers "how many times have I seen x?" in O(1) average, so the whole tally is O(n). Everything after this works off the d distinct (value, count) pairs, not the original n elements.

frames.caption: Count nums[4] = 2: freq[2] = 2.
frames.note: Step 1 of every top-K solution is the frequency map: one O(n) pass tallying value → count. A hash map answers "how many times have I seen x?" in O(1) average, so the whole tally is O(n). Everything after this works off the d distinct (value, count) pairs, not the original n elements.

frames.caption: Count nums[5] = 3: freq[3] = 1.
frames.note: Step 1 of every top-K solution is the frequency map: one O(n) pass tallying value → count. A hash map answers "how many times have I seen x?" in O(1) average, so the whole tally is O(n). Everything after this works off the d distinct (value, count) pairs, not the original n elements.

frames.caption: Frequency map done: { 1:3, 2:2, 3:1 }. Now bucket by count.
frames.note: With the tally complete we forget the original array and work only with the distinct (value, count) pairs. Allocate buckets with n+1 slots (indices 0..n): index = a possible frequency, slot = the values that hit exactly that frequency.

frames.caption: Value 1 occurs 3× → drop it into buckets[3].
frames.note: The bucket-sort insight: frequency is itself a small integer in [1, n], so use it as an ARRAY INDEX. Drop each value into buckets[count] — a counting-sort-style placement that is O(1) per value with no comparisons. Because a value can occur at most n times, an array of n+1 slots is guaranteed wide enough, which is what buys the O(n) (not O(n log n)) bound.

frames.caption: Value 2 occurs 2× → drop it into buckets[2].
frames.note: The bucket-sort insight: frequency is itself a small integer in [1, n], so use it as an ARRAY INDEX. Drop each value into buckets[count] — a counting-sort-style placement that is O(1) per value with no comparisons. Because a value can occur at most n times, an array of n+1 slots is guaranteed wide enough, which is what buys the O(n) (not O(n log n)) bound.

frames.caption: Value 3 occurs 1× → drop it into buckets[1].
frames.note: The bucket-sort insight: frequency is itself a small integer in [1, n], so use it as an ARRAY INDEX. Drop each value into buckets[count] — a counting-sort-style placement that is O(1) per value with no comparisons. Because a value can occur at most n times, an array of n+1 slots is guaranteed wide enough, which is what buys the O(n) (not O(n log n)) bound.

frames.caption: All values bucketed. Sweep frequencies from the highest index down, collecting values until result has k.
frames.note: The buckets now hold values sorted by frequency, implicitly, via their position. The highest non-empty index is the most frequent group. We never sorted anything — placement by index did the ordering for us.

frames.caption: f = 6: bucket empty, skip.
frames.note: This frequency has no values (no element occurred exactly that many times), so the slot is empty — skip it and keep sweeping down. Empty buckets are the price of indexing by frequency; skipping them is O(1) each and there are at most n+1 of them, so the sweep stays O(n) overall.

frames.caption: f = 5: bucket empty, skip.
frames.note: This frequency has no values (no element occurred exactly that many times), so the slot is empty — skip it and keep sweeping down. Empty buckets are the price of indexing by frequency; skipping them is O(1) each and there are at most n+1 of them, so the sweep stays O(n) overall.

frames.caption: f = 4: bucket empty, skip.
frames.note: This frequency has no values (no element occurred exactly that many times), so the slot is empty — skip it and keep sweeping down. Empty buckets are the price of indexing by frequency; skipping them is O(1) each and there are at most n+1 of them, so the sweep stays O(n) overall.

frames.caption: f = 3: take 1 → result = [1].
frames.note: Now read the answer off in frequency order for free: walk the buckets from the highest index DOWN, so the most frequent values come out first. Collect until we have k, then stop early — we never need to look at the lower buckets. This high-to-low sweep is the bucket-sort payoff: sorted-by-frequency output without ever calling a sort.

frames.caption: f = 2: take 2 → result = [1, 2]. Count == k, stop.
frames.note: Now read the answer off in frequency order for free: walk the buckets from the highest index DOWN, so the most frequent values come out first. Collect until we have k, then stop early — we never need to look at the lower buckets. This high-to-low sweep is the bucket-sort payoff: sorted-by-frequency output without ever calling a sort.

frames.caption: Answer = [1, 2] — the 2 most frequent values, in O(n) time and space.
frames.note: No sort, no heap: a frequency map (O(n)), counting-style bucket placement (O(n)), and one high-to-low sweep (O(n)) give the optimal O(n) batch solution. Reach for the size-k min-heap instead only when k is tiny next to n, or when elements stream in online and you cannot pre-allocate n+1 buckets.
